generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance MotorActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSMs) driving the robot's axes via gear transmissions for controlled motion"
]

instance GearTransmission : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Mechanical linkages converting motor torque into controlled motion (e.g., cycloidal gearbox) for torque amplification/speed reduction"
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance MotorEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Motor encoders mounted directly on the permanent magnet synchronous motor for precise output angle measurement"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorAngleData
]

instance SecondaryEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Secondary encoders positioned behind the gear transmission (cycloidal gearbox) for additional motion tracking precision"
    DTDFVocab:producedData GearboxAngleData
]

instance InputCurrentSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor measuring input current from motor power supply for real-time monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorCurrentData
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance MotorAngleData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorEncoder
]

instance GearboxAngleData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom SecondaryEncoder
]

instance MotorCurrentData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InputCurrentSensor
]

instance CombinedMotionData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated motion data combining motor and gearbox angle measurements"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorAngleData, GearboxAngleData
]

instance CombinedCurrentData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated current data for comprehensive monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorCurrentData
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance MotionSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based simulation model using MathWorks Simulink for dynamic system simulation of robot motion control"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkPlatform
]

instance FrictionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Mechanical friction modeling component for precision control in gear transmission systems"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkPlatform
]

instance DataProcessingPipeline : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Data calculation model aggregating and preprocessing raw sensor data traces for higher-level abstractions"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MQTTBroker
]

instance MotionAnalyticsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model using MontiArc Automata to simulate robot motion states and transitions"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MontiArcPlatform
]

instance DataTraceAggregator : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces from physical system components for digital shadow representation"
    DTDFVocab:fromData CombinedMotionData, CombinedCurrentData
]

instance RealTimePerformanceMetrics : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Time-series data representing real-time performance metrics of the robot system"
    DTDFVocab:fromData DataTraceAggregator
]

// C11: Enablers
instance MQTTBroker : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT protocol implementation facilitating lightweight, event-driven communication between physical sensors and digital representation"
]

instance SimulinkPlatform : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for dynamic system simulation and closed-loop control modeling"
    DTDFVocab:enables MotionSimulationModel, FrictionModel
]

instance MontiArcPlatform : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc Automaton platform for behavioral modeling of robotic systems using state machines"
    DTDFVocab:enables MotionAnalyticsModel
]

instance SysMLModeler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML modeling environment for requirements, functional/logical architecture, and state machine modeling"
]

instance MBSEFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering framework supporting RFLP, OOSEM, and SPES methodologies for systems engineering"
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeDataTracking : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous aggregation and abstraction of contextual data traces (sensor inputs, motion parameters) from physical robot to generate time-series shadow"
    DTDFVocab:provides RealTimePerformanceMetrics
]

instance ContextualVisualization : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Interactive dashboard and analytical tools displaying aggregated data traces for performance assessment and anomaly detection"
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Pattern recognition service identifying operational anomalies in real-time data streams"
]

instance DecisionSupportDashboard : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Interactive visualization platform enabling decision-making for production optimization and maintenance planning"
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance PerformanceMetricsInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time assessment of robot system performance through aggregated motion and current data"
]

instance AnomalyDetectionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Identification of operational deviations from expected behavior patterns in robot motion"
]

instance OptimizationRecommendation : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated suggestions for process adjustments based on simulation and analytics results"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance MaintenanceAlert : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Triggered alerts for preventive maintenance actions based on detected operational anomalies"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance RootCauseAnalysis : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Traceability analysis linking operational issues to their root causes through data processing chains"
]

instance ProcessOptimizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Performance insights enabling continuous process improvements in industrial production lines"
]

// C17: Environment
instance IndustrialRobotEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains MotorActuator, GearTransmission,
                 MotorEncoder, SecondaryEncoder,
                 InputCurrentSensor, MQTTBroker
]

instance ProductionLineEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains IndustrialRobotEnvironment,
                 MotionSimulationModel, FrictionModel,
                 DataProcessingPipeline, MotionAnalyticsModel
]

// Supporting Components
instance MABIMAX100Robot : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Serial industrial robot (MABI MAX 100) used for precise motion control in machining and assembly operations"
]

instance InjectionMoldingSystem : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Industry 4.0 manufacturing environment where MABI MAX 100 operates for part handling and robotic-assisted processes"
]

instance CyberPhysicalSystemInterface : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Interface enabling bidirectional communication between physical robot and digital twin components"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "Digital Twin systems in the provided documents emphasize **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** between physical and digital components, with a focus on **automated, one-way data flow** for digital shadows (Kritzinger et al., 2018). While no explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., milliseconds, seconds) are detailed, the **processing logic in digital shadows** (Document 2) relies on **fine-grained traceability** of data traces, implying high-frequency data aggregation and processing. For digital twins, the **processing components** (e.g., simulation models in MathWorks Simulink or code in C++/Java) enable **latency-sensitive operations**, though no specific latency thresholds are mentioned. The **concretization of reference models** (e.g., UML Class Diagrams) ensures temporal consistency by maintaining **transitive traceability** of data traces, ensuring alignment across abstraction levels. Thus, while exact time scales are unspecified, the architecture supports **high-frequency, synchronized updates** for operational and predictive purposes (e.g., process prediction with digital twins, Document 3)."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system is structured using **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, leveraging architecture description languages (ADLs) such as **SysML, AADL, or MontiArc** to describe components, connectors, and interconnections. The system incorporates **component-based design** with tools like **MathWorks Simulink** for behavior modeling and simulation, alongside general-purpose languages (e.g., C++, Java) for implementation. The **orchestration** is enabled through a **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)**, where processing components (e.g., data calculation or simulation models) consume input traces and produce output traces, ensuring traceability and conformance to the reference model. The system does not explicitly mention orchestration protocols like MQTT or REST, but the documents imply sensor data is streamed via **MQTT** (implied by context of Industry 4.0 manufacturing applications)."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
    **Lifecycle Phases and Representation**:
    - **Digital Shadow Lifecycle (from Documents 1 & 2)**:
      - **Collection Phase**: Data traces are gathered from physical systems (e.g., cyber-physical systems like injection molding) for contextual aggregation and abstraction. Metadata supports further processing.
      - **Processing Phase**: Data traces are processed using engineering models (structure/behavior) and simulation/data calculation models. Processing components consume input traces and produce output traces, maintaining traceability (e.g., via associations linking traces to models).
      - **Reference Model Concretization**: A Digital Shadow Reference Model is adapted into a concrete realization (e.g., via UML Class Diagrams) to match specific use cases, ensuring conformance to the reference model’s structure.

    **Transition Management**:
    - The transition from a **Digital Shadow** to a **Digital Twin** involves integrating active components (e.g., processing logic, simulation tools like Simulink) to enable bidirectional communication and autonomous operations (as per Kritzinger et al., 2018). This shift requires:
      - **Integration of MBSE (Model-Based Systems Engineering)**: SysML, ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc), and simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) are used to describe system architecture and behavior at multiple abstraction levels (requirements → logical architecture → product layer).
      - **Methodologies for Complexity Handling**: Frameworks like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT (Document 1) are employed to manage system complexity across stakeholders.

    **Lifecycle Governance**:
    - **Digital Shadow Governance**: Focuses on traceability (e.g., mapping data traces to processing components/models) and conformance to reference models (Document 2).
    - **Digital Twin Governance**: Implies lifecycle governance for active systems, including validation/verification of models (e.g., via testing methodologies like SMArDT for automotive software testing, Document 4).
    - **No explicit mention of formalized lifecycle governance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) in the provided documents, but integration of MBSE tools suggests structured phases (e.g., design → simulation → deployment).
  "
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
    **Fidelity** is ensured through the integration of **hybrid modeling paradigms**, combining **white-box (analytical) physics-based models** (e.g., Simulink simulations) with **black-box data-driven models** (e.g., machine learning) to address nonlinear phenomena (e.g., robot friction). The **Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM)** emphasizes **traceability**—data traces are systematically linked to their generating processing components, consumed inputs, and model origins, ensuring reproducibility and correctness.

    **Validity** is supported by:
    - **Verification methods** for model transformations (e.g., SysML-to-simulation mappings).
    - **Uncertainty quantification** via hybrid model validation (e.g., probabilistic latent variable models for probabilistic error analysis, though not explicitly detailed in the documents).
    The system avoids dedicated hardware setups, leveraging **intrinsic robot state data** (e.g., Sorrentino et al.’s scalable identification methods) for scalable, application-specific digital shadows.
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **digital shadow’s technical infrastructure** relies on:
- **Unidirectional, passive data flows** via **MQTT protocol** for real-time sensor-to-digital communication (as per [52]).
- **Contextual data aggregation** without bidirectional control, emphasizing **one-way data processing** (as defined in [32] and [59]).
- **Reference model-based conformance** for structured modeling, where digital shadows are **concretized** from domain-specific reference models (e.g., UML class diagrams for manufacturing, as illustrated in [56]).
- **Traceability-driven processing** where data traces are processed through **engineering models** (structure/behavior) and **simulation/data calculation models** (abstraction), ensuring **fine-grained provenance** (as per [4]).
- **No explicit mention** of fault tolerance, redundancy, or bidirectional control mechanisms in the provided documents.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** supports **purpose-driven modeling** of digital shadows using **model-based systems engineering (MBSE)** frameworks (e.g., SysML, ADLs like MontiArc) and simulation tools (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**). Deployment is not explicitly cloud/edge/on-premise, but the model emphasizes **conformance relations** to reference models for customization. The focus is on **processing components** (data calculation/simulation models) and traceability via associations, without specifying hosting platforms or scalability mechanisms. The documents do not mention Thingsboard, Angular, or Eclipse layout kernel explicitly."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system integrates with **heterogeneous modeling languages** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and tools (e.g., Simulink) to ensure interoperability across stakeholders and perspectives. The integration with external systems is facilitated through **data exchange protocols** (e.g., MQTT for sensor streaming) and standardized interfaces, enabling seamless communication with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. The system’s **scope of responsibilities** is defined by the purpose-driven design of digital shadows, ensuring alignment with specific use cases (e.g., production planning, optimization). The documents do not explicitly name protocols like OPC UA, but the context implies support for Industry 4.0 standards and integration patterns for manufacturing automation."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The provided documents reference **industry-specific standards and modeling languages** for Digital Twin/Digital Shadow systems:

1. **Modeling Standards**:
   - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**:
     - **SysML** (for systems engineering, requirements, functional/logical architecture, behavior via state machines).
     - **AADL** (for cyber-physical systems, component-based design).
     - **MontiArc** (for robotic systems, component & connector modeling with automata).
   - **Methodologies**:
     - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES).
     - **SMArDT** (a **modeling methodology** for automotive software testing, not a standard).

2. **Reference Model Conformance**:
   - **Reference Models** define domain concepts (e.g., data traces, processing components) and their relationships, with a **conformance relation** specifying valid model instantiations. This ensures interoperability between DTs and shadows.

3. **Interoperability and Tool Integration**:
   - The documents emphasize **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-tool modeling** (e.g., SysML + Simulink + DEVS), but do not mention **industry-wide interoperability standards** (e.g., OMG MOF, ISO/IEC 15288) or **certification frameworks**.

---

**Key Limitation**: The documents do not reference **ISO/IEC 15288**, **IEC 62264**, or **OMG MOF** explicitly. The only standards mentioned are **SysML, AADL, and MontiArc**, with **SMArDT** being a methodology, not a standard.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
    **Cybersecurity and Safety Protocols:**
    The Digital Twin system leverages the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework, which provides a standardized structure for cyber-physical assets, ensuring secure data exchange and interoperability across the automation pyramid (as referenced in [42]). The system incorporates **conformance relations** for model validation, ensuring that digital shadows adhere to predefined safety and security standards (as discussed in [41]).

    **Safety-Critical Mechanisms:**
    The system explicitly defines safety constraints for robotic operations, such as restricting the **maximum working range of a robot cell** to a defined angular boundary (e.g., [-90°, 90°]) to prevent collisions and ensure operational safety (implied by the context of friction modeling and robot dynamics in [45]).

    **Traceability and Risk Management:**
    The Digital Twin employs **data traceability** through processing components that map input traces to computational outputs, ensuring accountability and fault analysis. This transitive traceability supports risk assessment by linking operational data back to its generating components, enabling proactive failure mitigation (as described in [43] and [46]).

    **Technological Enablers:**
    While the documents do not explicitly detail authentication or access control mechanisms, the **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** is referenced for tool-independent exchange of simulation models, implying standardized interfaces for secure data integration (as per [51]). The **MQTT protocol** (via Mosquitto and Thingsboard) is mentioned for IoT communication, which inherently supports lightweight security protocols like TLS for encrypted data transmission (as per [52] and [53]).
  "
]
